title: Slope fields & equations
description: Work on the relationship between differential equations and the slope field that represents them.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which differential equation generates the slope field?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb700256bd157f03f84691ee76ad43dc.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: For this particular slope field, the segments that appear on the $\displaystyle~x$-axis (when $\displaystyle~y=0~$ are all vertical indicating infinite slope.  Hence, the two choices with $\displaystyle~y~$ in the denominator are possibilities.  Both of those choices also have $\displaystyle~x~$ in the numerator. That is consistent with the horizontal segments on the $\displaystyle~y$-axis.

Thus now we consider only the last two possibilities.

Look at the graph one quadrant at a time.  

In the first quadrant of the graph, where $\displaystyle~x~$ and $\displaystyle~y~$ are both positive, the slopes are negative.  This is consistent with $\displaystyle\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{x}{y}\,$, but **not** with $\displaystyle\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x}{y}\,$.

Checking the other three quadrants confirms that $~\displaystyle\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{x}{y}~$ is the correct answer.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which differential equation generates the slope field?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d48cb96aee0fb1f603f0cb37137409b4.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
For this particular slope field, the segments that appear along the line $\displaystyle~y=-x~$ are all horizontal and thus satisfy $~\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$. If we make this substitution into each of the choices, only  $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=x+y~$ gives an output of $\displaystyle~0~$ when $\displaystyle~y=-x\,$.

For further evidence, consider the line $\displaystyle~y=-x-1\,$.  If we substitute into the differential equation, we get $~\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=-1\,$.  Now check the slope field and observe that each of the short line segments along the line $~y=-x-1~$ appears to have a slope of $\displaystyle~-1\,$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which differential equation generates the slope field?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0d84181bf6c6ea70de96196c03fe9853.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: For this particular slope field, the segments that appear along the line $\displaystyle~y=x~$ are all horizontal and thus satisfy the condition $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$. If we make this substitution into each of the choices, only the two differential equations $~\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=x-y~$ and $~\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=y-x~$ give an output of $\displaystyle~0~$ when $\displaystyle~y=x\,$.

To decide between these two possibilities,  consider the line $\displaystyle~y=x-1\,$.  Substitute $\displaystyle~x-1~$ for $\displaystyle~y~$ in the two possible differential equations.

In $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=x-y\,$, we get
 $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=x-(x-1)=1\,$. That agrees with the slope field pictured.

In $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=y-x\,$, we get
 $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=(x-1)-x=-1\,$. That does **not** agree with the slope field.

The correct answer is  $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=x-y\,$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which differential equation generates the slope field?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e174b4fc7058e8b9d90e4a611c7db4ba.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: For this particular slope field, the segments that appear along the line $\displaystyle~y=2x~$ are all horizontal and thus satisfy the condition that$\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$. If we make this substitution into each of the choices, only  $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=2x-y~$ gives an output of $\displaystyle~0~$ when $~y=2x\,$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which differential equation generates the slope field?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d8af34be817d9d5cdefc17d8fce91ea0.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
For this particular slope field, whenever $\displaystyle~x=-1\,$, we have $~\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$. In addition, whenever $\displaystyle~y=0\,$, the slope is undefined (the slope field has a **vertical** segment at each point on the $\displaystyle~x$-axis).

These two observations suggest that the appropriate differential equation will have a factor of $\displaystyle~x+1~$ in the numerator and a factor of $\displaystyle~y~$ in the denominator.
Only $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x+1}{y}~$ meets these two conditions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which slope field is generated by the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=x-y$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The easiest way to attack this problem is to realize that the segments are horizontal when $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$, i.e., when $\displaystyle~x-y=0\,$.  

Only the third choice has horizontal segments along the line $\displaystyle~y=x\,$.

choices.content: ![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. There is a row of vertical slope segments on the x axis and a column of horizontal slope segments on the y axis. Above the x axis and from left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from positive slope to zero slope at the y axis, then to negative slope after. Below the x axis and from left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope at the y axis, then to positive slope after. The slope segments are steeper the closer they are to the x axis and the further they are from the y axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d7b706ae94d5dc4c9d04e5cf7b1e18df.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. There is a row of vertical slope segments on the x axis and a column of horizontal slope segments on the y axis. Above the x axis and from left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope at the y axis, then to positive slope after. Below the x axis and from left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from positive slope to zero slope at the y axis, then to negative slope after. The slope segments are steeper the closer they are to the x axis and the further they are from the y axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/073ec262a56d3fb9409c9d80241dbcd3.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d1946c9a65a3999ffba982a4c60bd968.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = negative x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffaa558aa0111ced1b8b9e6400a0b98d.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. There is a row of horizontal slope segments on the x axis and a column of vertical slope segments on the y axis. Above the x axis and from left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to vertical at the y axis, then to positive slope after. Below the x axis and from left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from positive slope to vertical at the y axis, then to negative slope after. The slope segments are steeper the farther they are from the x axis and the closer they are to the y axis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4a58689e0c873f5bad2f3c814c0ae697.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which slope field is generated by the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=2x-y$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The easiest way to attack this problem is to realize that the segments are horizontal when $~\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$, i.e., when $\displaystyle~2x-y=0\,$.  

Only the first choice has horizontal segments along the line $\displaystyle~y=2x\,$.

choices.content: ![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. The field has alternating rows from top to bottom. From left to right, the slope segments in the first gradually change from near vertical negative slope to 45 degree negative slope, then jump to 45 degree positive slope and gradually change back toward vertical. The slope segments in the second gradually change from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. The field achieves zero slope where y = 2 x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ba8e90a640a01b4ab8a89f7416e9b4b1.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d1946c9a65a3999ffba982a4c60bd968.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = negative x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffaa558aa0111ced1b8b9e6400a0b98d.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually decreases its negative slope, jumps to positive slope, then gradually increases its positive slope. Alternating rows achieves a lower degree of negative slope before changing to positive and at different x and y values, with no regular pattern.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a5312b9626ec05b79458d4831dc6b8f5.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = negative 2 x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/052b078378c490faeffca63d7d917196.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which slope field is generated by the differential equation $\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=x+y - 2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The easiest way to attack this problem is to realize that the segments are horizontal when $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,$, i.e., when $~x+y-2=0\,$.  

Only the second choice has horizontal segments along the line $\displaystyle~y=-x+2\,$.

choices.content: ![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = negative x minus 2.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0c8eb98864dd739be59b6aa71ca77ae2.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = negative x + 2.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fc7c9461ba5e1f509ee192b79d808007.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from near vertical negative slope to 45 degree negative slope, then jump to 45 degree positive slope and gradually change back toward vertical. Each row achieves negative 45 degree slope at y = negative x, and positive 45 degree slope at y = negative x + 2. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c4d678e0fba27fa622da761052f7ec5.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope where y = negative x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffaa558aa0111ced1b8b9e6400a0b98d.png)

![A slope field is plotted in the coordinate plane. From left to right, each row of slope segments gradually changes from negative slope to zero slope, then to positive slope. Each row achieves zero slope at y = one half negative x.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/81b3d0046f7eda1d61108c6248377f50.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which slope field is generated by the differential equation $\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{x}{y}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The easiest way to attack this problem is to realize that the short segments used to create the slope field are horizontal when $\displaystyle~ x=0\,$, i.e., when the point is on the $\displaystyle~y$-axis.  Similarly, the segments are vertical when $\displaystyle~y=0\,$, i.e. when the point is on the $\displaystyle~x$-axis.  Only the first choice has these characteristics.  

In addition, notice that the slope of a line through the origin and the point $\displaystyle~(x,y)~$ is $\displaystyle~\frac{y}{x}\,$.   Since $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{x}{y}\,$, the short segments we use to create the slope field at a given point are perpendicular to the line through the origin at that point.  This describes a circle through the origin which is also the set of solution curves for the first graph.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which slope field is generated by the differential equation $\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x-2}{y}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that $~\displaystyle \frac{dy}{dx} = 0~$ if and only if $~x=2\,$. 

The only slope field that fulfills that condition is the last one.

In addition, note that the last slope field has vertical segments along the $\displaystyle~x$-axis, i.e., where $\displaystyle~y=0\,$. That matches the differential equation, with a factor of $\displaystyle~y~$ in the denominator.

assessment_items.question_content:
In drawing the slope field for the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=y-2x\,$, I would place short line segments at select points on the $\displaystyle~xy$-plane.

**Complete the sentences.**

At the point  $\displaystyle~(-1,1)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 1]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(0,2)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 2]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(2,3)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 3]] .
hints: We calculate the requested slopes by substitution of the given $\displaystyle~x$- and $\displaystyle~y$-coordinates into $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}\,$.

At $\displaystyle~(-1,1)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}=1-2(-1)=1+2=3$

At $\displaystyle~(0,2)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}=2-2(0)=2$


At $\displaystyle~(2,3)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}=3-2(2)=3-4=-1$

assessment_items.question_content:
In drawing the slope field for the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=2x-3y\,$, I would place short line segments at select points on the $\displaystyle~xy$-plane.

**Complete the sentences.**

At the point  $\displaystyle~(0,1)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 1]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(1, -1)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 2]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(2,-2)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 3]] .
hints: We calculate the requested slopes by substitution of the given $\displaystyle~x$- and $\displaystyle~y$-coordinates into $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}\,$.

At $\displaystyle~(0,1)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad \frac{dy}{dx}=0 - 3=-3$

At $\displaystyle~(0,2)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad \frac{dy}{dx}=2+3=5$


At $\displaystyle~(2,3)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad \frac{dy}{dx}=4+6=10$

assessment_items.question_content:
In drawing the slope field for the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=xy\,$, I would place short line segments at select points on the $\displaystyle~xy$-plane.

**Complete the sentences.**

At the point  $\displaystyle~(1,2)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 1]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(3, 2)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 2]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(4,1)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 3]] .
hints: We calculate the requested slopes by substitution of the given $\displaystyle~x$- and $\displaystyle~y$-coordinates into $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}\,$.

At $\displaystyle~(1,2)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}= 1\cdot 2=2$

At $\displaystyle~(3,2)\,$:

$\displaystyle~\qquad \frac{dy}{dx}=3\cdot 2=6$


At $\displaystyle~(2,3)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad \frac{dy}{dx}=4\cdot 1=4$

assessment_items.question_content:
In drawing the slope field for the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=x+2y-2\,$, I would place short line segments at select points on the $\displaystyle~xy$-plane.

**Complete the sentences.**

At the point  $\displaystyle~(-2,0)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 1]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(0, 3)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 2]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(1, 1)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 3]] .
hints: We calculate the requested slopes by substitution of the given $\displaystyle~x$- and $\displaystyle~y$-coordinates into $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}\,$.

At $\displaystyle~(-2,0)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}= -2+0-2=-4$

At $\displaystyle~(0,3)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}= 0+6-2=4$


At $\displaystyle~(1,1)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad\frac{dy}{dx}= 1+2-2=1$

assessment_items.question_content:
In drawing the slope field for the differential equation $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}=3x-y+1\,$, I would place short line segments at select points on the $\displaystyle~xy$-plane.

**Complete the sentences.**

At the point  $\displaystyle~(-1, 3)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 1]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(1, 4)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 2]] .

At the point $\displaystyle~(2, 6)\,$, I would draw a short segment of slope [[☃ input-number 3]] .
hints: We calculate the requested slopes by substitution of the given $\displaystyle~x$- and $\displaystyle~y$-coordinates into $\displaystyle~\frac{dy}{dx}\,$.

At $\displaystyle~(-1,3)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad \frac{dy}{dx}=-3-3+1=-5$

At $\displaystyle~(1, 4)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad  \frac{dy}{dx}=3 - 4+1 = 0$


At $\displaystyle~(2, 6)\,$:

$\displaystyle\qquad  \frac{dy}{dx}=6-6+1=1$
